steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The phrase "fellow OECD member nation Spain" uses the country name as a restrictive appositive — it specifies which nation is meant, so no punctuation should separate "nation" from "Spain."

steps.title: Match to choices
steps.content:
The rule: a restrictive appositive that identifies which one must not be set off by any punctuation.
  • Choice A — nation Spain.: no punctuation between "nation" and "Spain" — the name sits directly against the noun it identifies. ✓
  • Choice B — nation, Spain.: a comma signals a non-restrictive (parenthetical) appositive, implying "nation" already identifies a unique referent — but here the name is needed to specify which member. ✗
  • Choice C — nation-Spain.: a hyphen joins words into a compound modifier, not an appositive; this construction is grammatically wrong here. ✗
  • Choice D — nation: Spain.: a colon introduces a list or explanation after an independent clause — "in fellow OECD member nation" is not an independent clause, so a colon is incorrect. ✗
A is correct.
